title: The Realization Effect: Risk-Taking after Realized versus Paper Losses
description: Study 1 (Lab) from Imas (2016). N=128 undergraduates play 4 rounds of a lottery investment game ($0-$2 per round, 1/6 chance of 7x win). After round 3, losses are either realized (handed over in cash) or remain on paper. Key finding: realized losses decrease subsequent risk-taking; paper losses increase it.

paper_tests.result_description: Participants who realized their losses decreased investment in round 4, while those with paper losses increased investment.
Non-parametric confirmation: paper loss group invests significantly more than realized loss group in round 4.
Participants who realized their losses decreased investment in round 4, while those with paper losses increased investment.
Non-parametric confirmation: paper loss group invests significantly more than realized loss group in round 4.
Participants who realized their losses decreased investment in round 4, while those with paper losses increased investment.
Non-parametric confirmation: paper loss group invests significantly more than realized loss group in round 4.

title: What You See Is All There Is
description: Six treatments from Enke (2020). Subjects estimate unknown quantities from partially observed signals. Treatments vary the signal selection mechanism (Selected, Control), signal complexity (Complex, Simple), and include a Nudge treatment and a Selected Replication. The naivete parameter χ measures neglect of unobserved information (0=Bayesian, 1=full neglect).

paper_tests.result_description: Subjects in the Selected treatment exhibit substantial correlation neglect (naivete ~0.48), while Control subjects show near-Bayesian updating (~-0.09).
Mann-Whitney confirmation: Selected treatment has significantly higher naivete than Control.
Subjects in the Selected treatment exhibit substantial correlation neglect (naivete ~0.48), while Control subjects show near-Bayesian updating (~-0.09).
Mann-Whitney confirmation: Selected treatment has significantly higher naivete than Control.
Subjects in the Selected treatment exhibit substantial correlation neglect (naivete ~0.48), while Control subjects show near-Bayesian updating (~-0.09).
Mann-Whitney confirmation: Selected treatment has significantly higher naivete than Control.

title: Cognitive Uncertainty — Risk A
description: Risk A experiment from Enke & Graeber (2023). Subjects state certainty equivalents for binary lotteries and report cognitive uncertainty. The probability weighting function shows the canonical inverse-S shape, and this effect is substantially stronger for high-CU decisions.

paper_tests.description: one-sample t-test (LLM-resolved): effect=-0.0125, p=0.0000 | The analysis requires a one-sample t-test to compare the normalized certainty equivalent (CE/y) at p=5% against the null hypothesis value of 0.05.
paper_tests.result_description: At low probabilities (p=5%), subjects overweight: normalized CE > p. One-sample t-test of CE/y against actuarially fair value 0.05.

paper_tests.description: one-sample t-test (LLM-resolved): effect=-0.0533, p=0.0000 | The analysis requires a one-sample t-test to compare the normalized certainty equivalent (CE/y) at p=95% against the null hypothesis value of 0.95.
paper_tests.result_description: At high probabilities (p=95%), subjects underweight: normalized CE < p. One-sample t-test of CE/y against actuarially fair value 0.95.

paper_tests.description: one-sample t-test (LLM-resolved): effect=-0.0000, p=0.0000 | The analysis requires a one-sample t-test to compare the normalized certainty equivalent (CE/y) against the null hypothesis value of 0.05 for the condition p05.
paper_tests.result_description: At low probabilities (p=5%), subjects overweight: normalized CE > p. One-sample t-test of CE/y against actuarially fair value 0.05.

paper_tests.description: one-sample t-test (LLM-resolved): effect=-0.0037, p=0.1046 | The analysis requires a one-sample t-test to compare the normalized certainty equivalent (CE/y) at high probabilities (p=95%) against the null hypothesis value of 0.95.
paper_tests.result_description: At high probabilities (p=95%), subjects underweight: normalized CE < p. One-sample t-test of CE/y against actuarially fair value 0.95.

paper_tests.description: one-sample t-test (LLM-resolved): effect=-0.0000, p=0.0000 | The statistical test required is a one-sample t-test comparing the normalized certainty equivalent (CE/y) at p=5% against the null hypothesis value of 0.05.
paper_tests.result_description: At low probabilities (p=5%), subjects overweight: normalized CE > p. One-sample t-test of CE/y against actuarially fair value 0.05.

paper_tests.result_description: At high probabilities (p=95%), subjects underweight: normalized CE < p. One-sample t-test of CE/y against actuarially fair value 0.95.

title: Cognitive Uncertainty — Beliefs A
description: Beliefs A experiment from Enke & Graeber (2023). Subjects perform balls-and-urns Bayesian updating tasks with varying base rates and signal diagnosticities. Reported beliefs show compression toward 50%, consistent with cognitive uncertainty causing people to shrink their beliefs toward the uninformative prior.

paper_tests.description: one-sample t-test (LLM-resolved): effect=-0.1352, p=0.0000 | The analysis requires a one-sample t-test comparing stated beliefs in the 'low_posterior' condition against the null hypothesis value of 0.15, as reported in the original paper.
paper_tests.result_description: At low Bayesian posteriors (~15%), stated beliefs are overestimated. One-sample t-test of stated belief against Bayesian posterior 0.15.

paper_tests.description: one-sample t-test (LLM-resolved): effect=-0.5776, p=0.0000 | The analysis requires a one-sample t-test comparing the stated belief against the Bayesian posterior value of 0.85 for the 'high_posterior' condition.
paper_tests.result_description: At high Bayesian posteriors (~85%), stated beliefs are underestimated. One-sample t-test of stated belief against Bayesian posterior 0.85.

paper_tests.description: one-sample t-test (LLM-resolved): effect=-0.0353, p=0.0169 | The analysis requires a one-sample t-test comparing the stated belief against the Bayesian posterior for the low_posterior condition, as reported in the original paper.
paper_tests.result_description: At low Bayesian posteriors (~15%), stated beliefs are overestimated. One-sample t-test of stated belief against Bayesian posterior 0.15.

paper_tests.description: one-sample t-test (LLM-resolved): effect=-0.0191, p=0.0864 | The analysis requires a one-sample t-test comparing stated beliefs against the Bayesian posterior of 0.85 specifically for the 'high_posterior' condition.
paper_tests.result_description: At high Bayesian posteriors (~85%), stated beliefs are underestimated. One-sample t-test of stated belief against Bayesian posterior 0.85.

paper_tests.description: one-sample t-test (LLM-resolved): effect=-0.0950, p=0.0000 | The analysis requires a one-sample t-test comparing the stated beliefs of the 'low_posterior' condition against the null hypothesis value of 0.15, as specified in the original paper.
paper_tests.result_description: At low Bayesian posteriors (~15%), stated beliefs are overestimated. One-sample t-test of stated belief against Bayesian posterior 0.15.

paper_tests.description: one-sample t-test (LLM-resolved): effect=-0.1937, p=0.0000 | The analysis requires a one-sample t-test to compare stated beliefs in the 'high_posterior' condition against the null hypothesis value of 0.85, consistent with the original paper's methodology.
paper_tests.result_description: At high Bayesian posteriors (~85%), stated beliefs are underestimated. One-sample t-test of stated belief against Bayesian posterior 0.85.

title: Motivated Errors — Adding Study
description: Adding Study from Exley & Kessler (2024). Subjects choose between an outside option and a charitable bundle. Adding a zero to the bundle reduces charity choice by 6pp when selfish motives are present (Self/Charity) but has no effect when both options benefit charity (Charity/Charity). Evidence of 'motivated errors'.

paper_tests.description: one-sample t-test (LLM-resolved): effect=0.1667, p=0.0000 | The analysis requires a one-sample t-test to compare the change in bundle choice rate (zero_effect) against the null hypothesis value of 0.
paper_tests.result_description: Adding a zero to a charity bundle reduces bundle choice by 6pp in Self/Charity (motivated reasoning condition).

paper_tests.description: one-sample t-test (LLM-resolved): effect=0.0833, p=0.0000 | The analysis requires a one-sample t-test to assess whether the zero_effect in the Charity/Charity condition is significantly different from zero.
paper_tests.result_description: Adding a zero has no effect in Charity/Charity (neutral condition), confirming the effect is driven by motivated reasoning.

paper_tests.description: one-sample t-test (LLM-resolved): effect=0.0150, p=0.3315 | The original paper used a one-sample t-test to compare the observed change in bundle choice rate against a null hypothesis of zero effect.
paper_tests.result_description: Adding a zero to a charity bundle reduces bundle choice by 6pp in Self/Charity (motivated reasoning condition).

paper_tests.description: one-sample t-test (LLM-resolved): effect=0.0150, p=0.3315 | The original paper used a one-sample t-test to assess the effect of adding a zero in the charity_charity condition against a null hypothesis value of 0.
paper_tests.result_description: Adding a zero has no effect in Charity/Charity (neutral condition), confirming the effect is driven by motivated reasoning.

paper_tests.description: one-sample t-test (LLM-resolved): effect=0.1667, p=0.0000 | The study used a one-sample t-test to compare the change in bundle choice rate against a null hypothesis of zero effect.
paper_tests.result_description: Adding a zero to a charity bundle reduces bundle choice by 6pp in Self/Charity (motivated reasoning condition).

paper_tests.description: one-sample t-test (LLM-resolved): effect=-0.0833, p=0.0013 | The analysis requires a one-sample t-test to compare the zero_effect in the Charity/Charity condition against the null hypothesis value of 0.
paper_tests.result_description: Adding a zero has no effect in Charity/Charity (neutral condition), confirming the effect is driven by motivated reasoning.

title: Motivated Errors — Correlation Neglect Study
description: Correlation Neglect Study from Exley & Kessler (2024). Subjects predict a number from correlated news channels. Correlation neglect (overweighting a common estimate) is exacerbated when selfish incentives align with the bias direction.

paper_tests.result_description: Overestimate condition shows more correlation neglect (larger negative error) than control condition.
Underestimate condition shows less correlation neglect than control, confirming directional asymmetry.
Overestimate condition shows more correlation neglect (larger negative error) than control condition.
Underestimate condition shows less correlation neglect than control, confirming directional asymmetry.
Overestimate condition shows more correlation neglect (larger negative error) than control condition.
Underestimate condition shows less correlation neglect than control, confirming directional asymmetry.

title: Motivated Errors — Anchoring Study A
description: Anchoring Study A from Exley & Kessler (2024). Subjects answer trivia questions with a low anchor (20). Slider default is the anchor value. Anchoring bias is exacerbated when selfish motives align with the bias direction.

paper_tests.result_description: Overestimate condition shows larger anchoring bias (more negative error) than control condition.
Underestimate condition shows less anchoring bias than control.
Overestimate condition shows larger anchoring bias (more negative error) than control condition.
Underestimate condition shows less anchoring bias than control.
Overestimate condition shows larger anchoring bias (more negative error) than control condition.
Underestimate condition shows less anchoring bias than control.

title: Motivated Errors — Anchoring Study B
description: Anchoring Study B from Exley & Kessler (2024). Same as Study A but subjects first judge above/below anchor, and slider starts at 50. Reduced anchoring bias but motivated-errors pattern persists.

paper_tests.result_description: Overestimate condition shows larger anchoring bias than control, even with direction-first design.
Underestimate condition shows less anchoring bias than control.
